subject: Hematology
title: Anemia
preambleMarkdown:
# Anemia



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Anemia is a clinical condition or syndrome in hematology.
- This article focuses on cell lineage, bleeding, thrombosis, hemolysis, marrow failure, transfusion, and malignancy patterns.
- The practical clinical priority is hematology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Anemia to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Anemia is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is hematology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Anemia depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In hematology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Anemia.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Anemia disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Anemia should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Anemia first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Anemia, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Anemia, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused hematology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Anemia presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Anemia with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Anemia is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Anemia should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Anemia is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses hematology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Anemia, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Anemia may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Anemia patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Anemia, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Anemia in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Anemia, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Anemia does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Anemia may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Anemia, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Anemia as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Anemia draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Anemias

_Source lines: 28946-29265._

```text
Anemias
Basics of Anemia
A. General Characteristics
1. Anemia is defined as a reduction in the number of circulating red
blood cells, as measured by hemoglobin (Hb) or hematocrit (Hct)
2. When red cell mass decreases, several compensatory mechanisms
maintain oxygen delivery to the tissues, including:
a. Increased cardiac output
b. Increased oxygen extraction ratio
c. Rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin curve (increased 2,3-
diphosphoglycerate [2,3-DPG])
d. Expansion of plasma volume
Quick HIT
Historical Findings to Consider in Patients With Anemia
- Family history (e.g., hemophilia, G6PD deficiency, thalassemia)
- Bleeding (e.g., recent trauma/procedure, hematemesis,
melena)
- Chronic illnesses (e.g., renal failure, autoimmune diseases,
malignancies)
- Ingestions (e.g., alcohol, medications)
1. Symptoms of anemia are highly variable, depending on the severity
of anemia, the rate at which it developed, and the underlying
oxygen demands of the patient. If anemia develops rapidly,
symptoms are more likely to be present, because there is little time

for physiologic compensation. If the onset is gradual, compensatory
mechanisms are able to maintain adequate oxygen delivery to
tissues, and symptoms may be minimal or absent
2. As a general rule, blood transfusion is not recommended unless
either of the following is true:
a. The Hb concentration is <7 g/dL OR
b. The patient requires increased oxygen-carrying capacity (e.g.,
patients with active coronary artery disease or other
cardiopulmonary disease)
B. Clinical Features

## 1. Fatigue

## 2. Dyspnea

## 3. Headache

## 4. Palpitations

## 5. Tachycardia

6. Pallor-best noted in the conjunctiva
7. Signs/symptoms of the underlying cause: orthostatic
lightheadedness, syncope, or hypotension if acute bleeding;
jaundice if hemolytic anemia; blood in stool if GI bleeding
Quick HIT
Pseudoanemia or dilutional anemia refers to a decrease in Hb and
Hct secondary to dilution (i.e., secondary to acute volume infusion
or overload). This is a diagnosis of exclusion; all cell lines on a
complete blood count should drop proportionally in the right clinical
context.

C. Diagnosis

## 1. Decreased Hb and Hct

a. Formula for converting Hb to Hct: Hb  3 = Hct (1 unit of
packed RBCs [PRBCs] increases Hb level by 1 point and Hct by
3 points).

b. Hb of 7 to 8 provides suficient oxygen-carrying capacity for
most patients-anemia is not tolerated as well in patients with
impaired cardiac function (see Clinical Pearl 9-1).

2. Reticulocyte index
a. Reports the number of reticulocytes present as a percentage of
the total number of RBCs.

b. A corrected reticulocyte index to account for degree of anemia
can be calculated (in states of anemia, reticulocyte production
should increase accordingly).

c. The reticulocyte count is an important initial test in evaluating
anemia because it indicates whether effective erythropoiesis is
occurring in the bone marrow.

d. Effective erythropoiesis is dependent on adequate raw materials
(iron, vitamin B 12 , folate) in the bone marrow, absence of
intrinsic bone marrow disease (e.g., aplastic anemia), adequate
erythropoietin from the kidney, and survival of reticulocytes (no
premature destruction before leaving the bone marrow)-see
Clinical Pearl 9-2.

e. A reticulocyte index >2% implies the bone marrow is
responding to increased RBC requirements
f. A reticulocyte index <2% implies inadequate RBC production
by the bone marrow
CLINICAL PEARL 9-1
Transfusion Pearls
- PRBCs (contain no platelets or clotting factors)
- Mix with normal saline to infuse faster (not with lactated Ringer
solution because calcium causes coagulation within the IV line)
- Each unit raises the hematocrit by 3 to 4 points
- Each unit may be given to an adult for over 90 to 120 minutes
- Always check CBC after the transfusion is completed
- FFP
- Contains all of the clotting factors
- Contains no RBCs/WBCs/platelets
- Given for high PT/PTT, coagulopathy, and deficiency of clotting
factors-FFP can be given if you cannot wait for vitamin K to take
effect, or if patient has liver failure (in which case vitamin K will not
work)
- Follow up PT and PTT to assess response
- Cryoprecipitate
- Contains factor VIII and fibrinogen
- For hemophilia A, decreased fibrinogen (DIC) and vWD
- Platelet transfusions-1 unit raises platelet count by 10,000
- Whole blood only for massive blood loss
- For any patient with massive blood loss, the ideal ratio of platelets: FFP:
PRBCs which are transfused should be 1:1:1. In addition, during massive
blood transfusions, blood should be warmed to prevent a decrease in
core body temperature.

Quick HIT

If Hb and Hct reveal anemia, the next tests to obtain to determine
cause of anemia are reticulocyte count and MCV.

Quick HIT
Note that all causes of anemia are initially normocytic because it
takes some time for the abnormal-sized RBCs to outnumber the
normal-sized ones.

CLINICAL PEARL 9-2
Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions Are Divided Into Intravascular
and Extravascular Hemolysis
- Intravascular hemolysis (also called acute hemolytic reactions):
- Very serious and life-threatening-caused by ABO-mismatched blood
transfused into patient (usually due to clerical error). For example, if B
blood is given to a type A patient, anti-B IgM antibodies attach to all
of the infused B RBCs, activate the complement pathway, and
produce massive intravascular hemolysis as C9 punches holes
through RBC membranes.

- Symptoms include fever/chills, nausea/vomiting, pain in the flanks/
back, chest pain, and dyspnea
- Complications include hypovolemic shock (hypotension, tachycardia),
DIC, and renal failure with hemoglobinuria
- Management involves stopping the transfusion immediately and
aggressively replacing the fluid to avoid shock and renal failure,
epinephrine for anaphylaxis, dopamine/norepinephrine as needed to
maintain blood pressure
- Extravascular hemolysis (also called delayed hemolytic transfusion
reaction):
- Extravascular hemolysis is less severe and in most cases is self-
limited; it may occur within 3 to 4 weeks after a transfusion
- It is caused by one of the minor RBC antigens. For example, if a
patient is Kell antigen-negative and has anti-Kell IgG antibodies from
a previous exposure to the antigen, reexposure of her memory B cells
to Kell antigen on RBCs will result in synthesis of IgG anti-Kell
antibodies. These antibodies coat all of the Kell antigen-positive
donor RBCs, which will be removed extravascularly by macrophages
in the spleen, liver, and bone marrow.

- Symptoms are subtle and include fever, jaundice, and anemia
- No treatment required-the prognosis is good

1. RBC indices
a. Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) is the average size of a
patient's RBCs
b. Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) is the average Hb content
of a patient's RBCs
c. Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) is the
average Hb concentration in each RBC
d. RDW is the variation in RBC size
2. Peripheral blood smear
D. Diagnosing the Cause of Anemia (General Approach)
1. If the reticulocyte index is <2%, indicating an inadequate bone
marrow response, examine the peripheral blood smear and RBC
indices (Figure 9-1)
a. If microcytic anemia (MCV <80), the differential diagnosis
includes the following:
- Iron-deficiency anemia-most common cause
- Anemia of chronic disease-iron is present in the body but is
not available for Hb synthesis due to iron-trapping in
macrophages
- Thalassemia-defective synthesis of globin chains
- Sideroblastic anemia (includes lead poisoning, pyridoxine
deficiency, toxic effects of alcohol)-defective synthesis of
protoporphyrins leading to iron accumulation in
mitochondria
f. If macrocytic anemia (MCV >100), the differential diagnosis
includes the following:
- Megaloblastic anemia due to vitamin B 12 , folate, or copper
deficiency (MCV increases significantly)-defective DNA
synthesis leading to nuclear defects in RBCs
- Liver disease (MCV increases up to 115)-abnormal
incorporation of plasma lipoproteins into RBC membranes
alters RBC shape and increases their volume
- Reticulocytosis (MCV increases up to 110)-due to
hemolysis, bleeding, etc. Reticulocytes are larger than
mature RBCs, resulting in an increase in polychromatophilic
RBCs. Associated with an increased RDW (multiple RBC
populations of differing size and age)
- Alcohol use disorder
- Hypothyroidism
l. If normocytic anemia (MCV 80 to 100), the differential
diagnosis includes the following:
- Aplastic anemia-bone marrow failure

- Bone marrow fibrosis
- Bone marrow infiltration
- Anemia of chronic disease
- Renal failure-decreased erythropoietin production results
in decreased erythropoiesis
- Nutrient deficiency (early stages of iron, vitamin B 12 , folate,
or copper deficiency)
2. If the reticulocyte index is >2%, indicating an adequate bone
marrow response, the differential diagnosis includes the following:
a. Acute blood loss
b. Hemolysis
FIGURE
9-1 Evaluation of anemia.

Quick HIT

The first step in evaluation of the anemic patient is to assess
volume status and hemodynamic stability, in order to identify any
active bleeding. If hemodynamically unstable, transfuse PRBCs
before attempting to find the source.

Microcytic Anemias
Iron-Deficiency Anemia
A. Causes
1. Chronic blood loss
2. Most common cause of iron-deficiency anemia in adults
3. Menstrual blood loss is the most common source-in the absence of
menstrual bleeding, GI blood loss is most likely
4. Dietary deficiency/increased iron requirements, which are
primarily seen in the following three age groups:
a. Infants and toddlers-occurs especially if the diet is
predominantly human milk, which is low in iron; children in
this age group also have an increased requirement for iron due
to accelerated growth
b. Adolescents-increased requirement for iron due to rapid
growth; adolescents who menstruate are particularly at risk due
to loss of menstrual blood
c. Pregnant individuals-pregnancy increases iron requirements
Quick HIT
In elderly patients with iron-deficiency anemia, you must rule out
colon cancer.

Quick HIT
Pica (the craving for nonfood items) and pagophagia (the craving
for ice) have been associated with iron-deficiency anemia.

B. Diagnosis
1. Iron studies (see Table 9-1)
a. Decreased serum ferritin
b. Increased TIBC/transferrin levels

c. Decreased transferrin saturation
d. Decreased serum iron
2. Peripheral blood smear-reveals microcytic, hypochromic RBCs
(Figure 9-2)
3. Bone marrow biopsy-reveals absence of stainable iron; this test is
technically the gold standard, but typically only indicated if
laboratory evidence of iron-deficiency anemia is present and no
source of blood loss is found
4. Test the stool for occult blood. Often may need to proceed directly
to endoscopy/colonoscopy if pre-test probability for GI bleeding is
high-colon cancer is a common cause of GI bleeding in those age
50 years or older
TABLE 9-1 Iron Studies in Microcytic Anemias
C. Treatment
1. Oral iron replacement (ferrous sulfate)
a. A trial of iron replacement therapy may be given to young,
otherwise healthy, menstruating individuals without
investigating for additional sources of blood loss (in
nonmenstruating and postmenopausal individuals, attempt to
determine the source of blood loss first)
b. Side effects include constipation, nausea, and dyspepsia
2. Parenteral iron replacement
a. There are multiple formulations of IV iron (ferric gluconate,
ferumoxytol, iron sucrose, iron dextran, others). All are equally
effective and have similar adverse effect profile (extremely rare
infusion reaction or anaphylaxis)
b. Preferred over oral iron for the following situations:
- Poor adherence, poor gut absorption, or GI side effects from
oral iron
- Severe iron deficiency or ongoing blood loss
- Inflammatory state that interferes with absorption of oral
iron
- Preference to rapidly replete iron instead of over several
months
3. Blood transfusion is not recommended unless Hb meets the

transfusion threshold (usually <7 g/dL, sometimes higher) or the
patient has cardiopulmonary disease
Quick HIT
The MCH and MCHC are neither sensitive nor specific for any
diagnosis. However, MCV is very valuable in diagnosing the cause
of anemia.

Quick HIT
The RDW measures the variation in RBC size. The RDW is usually
elevated in anemias caused by nutritional deficiencies. An
increased RDW is called anisocytosis.
```
